summary: This essay analyzes three cryptocurrency grant programs (Dash, Monero, and Zcash) to understand how open source projects allocate funding when they have it. Asparouhova examines the structural tradeoffs of each program's design—from centralized foundation grants to decentralized crowdfunding to blockchain-encoded budgets. She finds that funding primarily goes to core work, community building, marketing, and research, with a small group of trusted repeat contributors receiving most awards. The analysis reveals that projects don't need enormous budgets to be effective, but reputation and accountability mechanisms are crucial for successful grant programs.
key_points: Reputation is the primary signal for funding decisions—a small group of trusted contributors receives most funding across all programs, and this isn't controversial
Public application processes with pre-proposals reduce noise while leveraging community knowledge for better filtering
Different funding sources create different incentives: dedicated budgets may lead to superfluous spending, while crowdfunding makes long-term planning difficult
Not all valuable contributions require funding—some work benefits from being done voluntarily first, with compensation sought afterward
Core maintenance work makes up a disproportionate share of funded proposals compared to submitted proposals, suggesting grant programs effectively address the maintenance problem
money_quotes: If you have the money, you'll find ways to spend it, even if it's not really necessary.
Who works on a project seems just as important as the idea itself; if there isn't a good team to work on an idea, is the idea really worth debating?
Leaving it too open can turn funding into a popularity contest.
Core members are paid to work on the project, but their salaries are contingent on community approval and public accountability.
In open source, not everything that's valuable is immediately fundable, but that's not necessarily a bad thing.
Funding is ideally allocated towards regular contributors, maintainers, and people with 'known' reputations, rather than random or first-time contributors.
I came away feeling more certain that money can unlock so much more opportunity and stability for open source projects.
key_insight: Effective open source funding programs don't distribute money democratically to all contributors, but rather concentrate resources on a small group of trusted, accountable repeat contributors—and this concentration of funding is both functional and uncontroversial.
